question_title: What will US airline passenger volume be for these weeks in April 2026, according to the TSA? (Apr 13–19, 2026)
summary: TSA passenger volume for Apr 13-19, 2026 is forecast at approximately 17.1M, with a left-skewed distribution reflecting downside risk from the deepening jet fuel crisis. The most recent week (Apr 3-9) showed 17.38M, essentially flat vs 2024/2025 despite the Iran war and Strait of Hormuz disruptions. Historical baseline for this calendar week is 17.3-17.4M. The modest downward shift from recent levels reflects: (1) the crisis is deepening per multiple analyst warnings, (2) United Airlines cutting 5% of routes in Q2-Q3, and (3) jet fuel prices having nearly doubled. However, US domestic capacity is still growing (2.1%), limiting the magnitude of decline. The distribution has a meaningful left tail to account for potential further escalation in the Iran conflict.

factors.description: Recent TSA data (Apr 3-9) shows 17.38M weekly total, essentially flat vs 2024/2025 same-period despite ongoing fuel crisis
Jet fuel crisis deepening: prices doubled to $4.88/gal, 5% route cuts by United, 14.6% North American cancellations on peak day. Analysts warn crisis could deepen in coming weeks
US domestic capacity still growing 2.1% (down from 2.3%), not contracting - crisis impact primarily on international routes and select unprofitable domestic routes
2024 Apr 13-19 = 17.32M, 2025 Apr 13-19 = 17.42M (Easter-elevated). Historical baseline for this calendar week is ~17.3M
April 13-19 2026 is 1 week post-Easter (Easter was Apr 5), a normal non-holiday week with no spring break boost
Tail risk: if Strait of Hormuz closure escalates or new military escalation, could see sharper capacity cuts (5-10%)
factors.conditional: Major escalation in Iran conflict during Apr 10-19

reasoning:
**Forecast: TSA Passenger Volume Apr 13-19, 2026**

**Median estimate: ~17.1M passengers** (80% CI: 16.36M - 17.59M)

Key reasoning:
- The most recent complete week (Apr 3-9) showed **17.38M** passengers, nearly identical to the same period in 2024 (17.32M) and 2025 (17.42M)
- Despite the Iran war and jet fuel prices nearly doubling, TSA data has been remarkably stable — the crisis eroded ~7 percentage points of year-over-year growth but hasn't pushed volumes below historical norms
- The distribution is **left-skewed** because the crisis is deepening: United cutting 5% of routes, 14.6% of North American flights cancelled on one peak day, and analysts warning it could worsen
- US domestic capacity is still growing at 2.1%, which limits the downside — the crisis disproportionately affects international routes
- The 5th percentile (~16.0M) reflects the possibility of a sharp escalation; the 95th percentile (~17.7M) is bounded by the stalled growth environment

resolution_criteria:
This question is a subquestion of a group question. This subquestion specifically pertains to (and resolves according to) the option 'Apr 13–19, 2026'. The resolution criteria for the parent question is below. 

These subquestions resolve as the total volume of passengers screened by the Transportation Security Administration (TSA) at its checkpoints for the rolling 7-day periods in the listed options.

TSA posts the data at the following link:
[https://www.tsa.gov/travel/passenger-volumes](https://www.tsa.gov/travel/passenger-volumes)
fine_print: This question's information (resolution criteria, fine print, background info, etc) is synced with an [original identical question](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43112) which opened on 2026-04-06 23:04:00. This question will resolve based on the resolution criteria and fine print of the linked original question. However, if this question would resolve differently than the original question, then this question will be annulled. Additionally, if the original question's resolution could have been known before this question opened, then this question will be annulled.
